assembled.architecture: kagent is a Kubernetes-native AI-agent platform implemented as a single Go module (github.com/kagent-dev/kagent/go, Go 1.26.3) split into three layers: a kubebuilder-style CRD API package, a controller-runtime operator, and a Google-ADK agent runtime. The control plane is a controller-runtime operator (sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime v0.24.1) that reconciles CRDs (Agent, SandboxAgent, ModelConfig, ModelProviderConfig, RemoteMCPServer, AgentHarness; 8 CRDs generated by controller-gen) into rendered Kubernetes workloads, plus a gorilla/mux v1.8.1 HTTP API server (wrapped in otelhttp for OpenTelemetry tracing) that serves on all replicas. The single persistent store is PostgreSQL accessed through jackc/pgx/v5 (pgxpool) with a sqlc v1.30.0-generated repository (queries hand-authored in queries/*.sql, Go code-generated), pgvector for 768-dim agent-memory embeddings, and golang-migrate schema migrations over two tracks (core + optional vector). Validation is two-tiered: declarative kubebuilder/CEL XValidation rules run at apiserver admission, with a small amount of imperative Go validation for constraints CEL cannot express. The agent runtime (adk/) is a separate dataplane library that wires a Google ADK llmagent (google.golang.org/adk) into the A2A protocol (a2a-go) and persists via the controller's REST API rather than a direct DB connection. Tests use stretchr/testify plus envtest (a real ephemeral kube-apiserver) to pin CEL admission rules end-to-end; structured logging is logr/zapr; sandbox workloads run over an external Substrate ate-api gRPC control plane.

components.name: api (CRD types, v1alpha1/v1alpha2)
components.role: Kubebuilder-style CRD schema package (group kagent.dev): per-type SchemeBuilder self-registration, kubebuilder + CEL XValidation admission rules, and a few imperative Go validators; v1alpha2 is the storage version, v1alpha1 served-but-not-stored with no conversion webhooks.

components.name: core/controller+translator
components.role: The controller-runtime reconciliation + ADK-translation layer: idempotent owned-object create-or-update with set-difference pruning, cross-namespace authorization, secret-hash-driven rollout, DAG enforcement of the agent tool graph, and DB persistence of rendered config.

components.name: core/httpserver
components.role: The gorilla/mux HTTP API (sessions/events/tasks/memory/modelconfig/toolservers/agentharness gateway): ordered auth->logging->error middleware, pluggable authn/authz (insecure-by-default), in-process semantic dry-run validation, and Postgres-backed handlers behind a shared Base DI struct.

components.name: core/database
components.role: The sqlc-generated, pgx/v5-pool-backed PostgreSQL persistence layer: withTx atomicity, ON-CONFLICT upserts with soft-delete (and resurrection) conventions, user-id query scoping, pgvector memory search/TTL pruning, and golang-migrate migrations with data-loss guards.

components.name: core/sandboxbackend
components.role: Sandbox/agent-runtime backend abstraction: a RoutingBackend dispatching on spec.platform between an agent-sandbox CRD path and an external Substrate (ate-api gRPC) control plane, with idempotent actor lifecycle, deterministic actor IDs, digest-pinned images, and a default-deny L7 egress allowlist.

components.name: adk/agent-runtime
components.role: Google ADK agent runtime/dataplane: constructs an llmagent run inside an A2A server, persists sessions/events/tasks/memory via the controller's REST API, and owns the per-request execution loop, HITL tool-approval, and pgvector long-term memory — consumed by the controller, not itself a reconciler.

critical_to_know.claim: Owned-object reconciliation is idempotent via a custom CreateOrUpdate that calls reconcilerutils.ObjectsEqual (a proto-aware DeepEqual skipping ObjectMeta/TypeMeta/Status) and ONLY issues an Update when relevant fields differ (OperationResultNone otherwise) — the load-bearing no-op guard preventing reconcile hot-loops on proto-based CRDs that reflect.DeepEqual mis-compares.

Pruning is authoritative by set-difference: FindOwnedObjects lists all objects owned by the Agent UID, reconcileDesiredObjects deletes each desired object from that map, and whatever remains is actively deleted — so an object dropping out of the rendered manifest IS deleted on the next reconcile. This depends on SetupOwnerIndexes only indexing owners of Kind 'Agent'/'SandboxAgent'; a new owner type silently violates pruning with no compile-time signal.

Secret-rotation rollout is driven by a content hash, not the Secret name: computeStatusSecretHash hashes namespaced-name + sorted data keys/values into Status.SecretHash (ModelConfig, RemoteMCPServer), folded into the agent config hash, so an in-place CA/cert rotation (same name, new PEM) forces a Deployment roll — without it an agent keeps dialing with stale cached trust.

Reconcile ordering per Agent is fixed: validateCrossNamespaceReferences -> CompileAgent -> BuildManifest -> (optional mutate) -> reconcileDesiredObjects (apply+prune) -> upsertAgent (DB persist LAST), so the DB only reflects successfully-applied manifests. If pruning fails, upsertAgent is skipped — a window where the live Deployment and DB-stored config can diverge until a later successful reconcile.

The agent tool graph is enforced as a DAG at translate time: validateAgent tracks visitedAgents (kind-qualified) plus a depth counter MAX_DEPTH=10 — a revisit returns 'cycle detected in agent tool chain', depth>10 returns 'recursion limit reached', self-reference is separately rejected. Cycle/recursion protection lives in the translator, NOT the CRD.

MutateFuncFor merges annotations/labels over existing but REPLACES Deployment/Sandbox/PodTemplate spec wholesale (existing.Spec = desired.Spec) because mergo does not reliably replace slice fields (containers/volumes/env) — without the explicit assignment a spec change would not roll pods (a workaround copied from the opentelemetry-operator). mutateDeployment only sets Replicas when desired is non-nil, a deliberate HPA-compatibility carve-out.

ModelConfigSpec admission enforces ~16 CEL XValidation discriminated-union rules: each provider sub-block (openAI/anthropic/azureOpenAI/ollama/gemini/geminiVertexAI/anthropicVertexAI/bedrock/sapAICore) must be nil unless self.provider matches — the canonical tagged-union admission guard repeated per provider.

CEL credential/security invariants on ModelConfigSpec: apiKeySecret required if apiKeySecretKey set; apiKeySecretKey required if apiKeySecret set EXCEPT Bedrock and SAPAICore; apiKeyPassthrough is mutually exclusive with apiKeySecret and must be false for Gemini/GeminiVertexAI/AnthropicVertexAI; openAI.tokenExchange requires apiKeySecret and excludes apiKeyPassthrough.

Cross-namespace security (CEL, non-obvious): RemoteMCPServerSpec rejects a CA-pinning Secret (spec.tls.caCertSecretRef) together with a cross-namespace-permitting allowedNamespaces (from: All/Selector), because the pinned CA Secret is mounted by bare name onto the CONSUMING agent's pod and resolves in the agent's namespace, not the RemoteMCPServer's — so a CA-pinning server cannot be referenced cross-namespace. It also rejects spec.tls when spec.url is http://.

TLSConfig CEL trust rules live on the TYPE (inherited by ModelConfig and RemoteMCPServer): caCertSecretKey<->caCertSecretRef both-or-neither, and disableSystemCAs requires caCertSecretRef or disableVerify ('trust-nothing config rejects every upstream'); a dedicated envtest pins these against a real apiserver to catch silent drops on controller-gen upgrades.

Cross-namespace authorization is enforced before translation in validateCrossNamespaceReferences: same-namespace refs always allowed; the target namespace must be in the controller's watchedNamespaces; the target Agent/RemoteMCPServer must opt in via AllowedNamespaces.AllowsNamespace; MCPServer and Service are HARD-banned cross-namespace. An unauthorized ref fails reconcile with a Failed condition.

Middleware order is load-bearing and first-registered runs OUTERMOST: wsAuthQueryMiddleware -> AuthnMiddleware -> contentTypeMiddleware -> loggingMiddleware -> errorHandlerMiddleware — auth runs before logging/error-handling, and the wsAuth query->header mapping must run before authentication (WebSocket clients cannot set headers).

INSECURE-BY-DEFAULT auth: the default UnsecureAuthenticator trusts client-supplied identity (user_id query / X-User-Id header), falling back to a hard-coded 'admin@kagent.dev' when absent, and the default NoopAuthorizer.Check always returns nil. If a deployment keeps these defaults, every unauthenticated request runs as admin with full access. The ProxyAuthenticator additionally decodes JWT claims WITHOUT signature verification, trusting an upstream proxy.

Data isolation is enforced at the DB query layer by threading userID into every query (GetSession/DeleteSession/ListSessions take userID; session, event, checkpoint, crewai and memory reads/writes all key on user_id) — there is no separate handler-level ownership check; a wrong userID simply returns not-found. The only cross-user read is the explicit ListSessionsForAgentAllUsers.

ATOMICITY: every multi-statement DB operation goes through withTx(ctx, fn) — begin tx, defer Rollback, run fn against a tx-scoped Queries, commit only on success (deferred Rollback after Commit is a harmless no-op). This is the ONLY atomicity boundary in the layer; the DBTX seam lets identical sqlc-generated methods run pooled (autocommit) or transactional with no duplication.

UPSERT RESURRECTION ASYMMETRY (easy-to-miss trap): agent/tool/toolserver/crewai-memory ON-CONFLICT upserts set deleted_at = NULL (resurrecting a soft-deleted row), but UpsertSession does NOT reset deleted_at — re-storing a soft-deleted session leaves it deleted/invisible. A 'store' can appear to succeed yet the row stays hidden (Get/List filter deleted_at IS NULL).

SOFT-DELETE is the dominant convention (delete = SET deleted_at=NOW(); every Get/List carries deleted_at IS NULL), with deliberate EXCEPTIONS: the pgvector memory table and crewai_agent_memory use HARD DELETE. StoreEvents inserts a batch in a plain for-loop with NO surrounding transaction (each InsertEvent autocommits), an atomicity gap vs StoreAgentMemories/StoreCheckpointWrites which do wrap in withTx.

Memory TTL/prune is order-dependent inside one transaction: PruneExpiredMemories runs ExtendMemoryTTL FIRST (rows expires_at<NOW() AND access_count>=10 get +15 days and access_count reset to 0) THEN DeleteExpiredMemories (expires_at<NOW() AND access_count<10) — popular rows are rescued before the delete pass; the >=10 vs <10 split is the load-bearing retention threshold.

Migration data-loss guard: on a failed Up, applyDir rolls back ONLY when prevVersion!=0; when prevVersion==0 (no migration ever applied) rollback is SKIPPED so a failed first migration does not DROP pre-existing GORM-AutoMigrated tables. The baseline 000001 is reverse-engineered to match GORM AutoMigrate output (nullable created_at/updated_at, BIGINT version/access_count), so new code must treat timestamps as nullable.

ADK event persistence uses a DETACHED context (context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30s)) deliberately so a client disconnect does not cancel the in-flight backend write; the request ctx is intentionally NOT propagated. AppendEvent persists remote FIRST then updates the in-memory localSession (read-your-writes ordering), and partial/streaming events store nothing.

Substrate actor deletion is a state machine performing AT MOST ONE mutating ate-api step per call, requiring the caller to requeue until done=true: a RUNNING/RESUMING actor is Suspended (not deleted), a SUSPENDING actor is left to settle, and only a SUSPENDED/UNSPECIFIED actor is DeleteActor'd; NotFound at any stage is treated as success so the finalizer removes idempotently. Substrate workload images MUST be digest-pinned (pinImageRef rejects any ref lacking '@').

OpenClaw/NemoClaw sandbox egress is a default-deny L7 allowlist: only clawhub.ai/openclaw.ai (GET+POST) and docs.openclaw.ai (GET only) are permitted by baseline, each bound to specific binary paths; Telegram/Slack egress is added only when a matching channel is configured. The filesystem is read-only by default (small ReadWrite set) and the process runs as the unprivileged 'sandbox' user.

Substrate insecure-TLS gap: Config.Insecure sets tls.InsecureSkipVerify=true on the ate-api gRPC client while the bearer token is still sent (requireTLS gated on !Insecure), so in insecure mode the per-RPC bearer credential's RequireTransportSecurity()==false lets the auth token ride a connection whose peer identity is unverified. Intended for Kind/local, but there is no guard preventing Insecure=true in a real cluster config.

ModelConfig create/update use OPPOSITE ordering rationales to bound partial-failure windows: CREATE writes the CR then API-key Secret then companion Secrets, rolling back (Delete the CR) if companion-Secret creation fails; UPDATE writes Secrets BEFORE flipping the Spec so a partial failure leaves the CR pointing at its prior still-valid Secret layout. Stale-Secret sweeps are best-effort and never fail the PUT.

The AgentHarness gateway reverse proxy injects its own upstream credentials (a resolved Bearer token + hard-coded 'x-openclaw-scopes: operator.admin') and rewrites Origin/Referer to loopback, but performs NO Check(h.Authorizer,...) per-resource authorization before proxying browser traffic — unlike the resource handlers which all gate on Check(). Access control depends entirely on the authn middleware having established a principal.

extractions.role: api-types / CRD schema package for a Kubernetes controller

facts.claim: The CRD API types are split into two versioned Go packages, v1alpha1 and v1alpha2, both under group `kagent.dev`. v1alpha2 is the newer/storage version (Agent, ModelConfig, and ModelProviderConfig carry `+kubebuilder:storageversion`); v1alpha1 has no storageversion marker, so it is a served-but-not-stored legacy version.

There are NO conversion webhooks between v1alpha1 and v1alpha2 — no `Hub()`, `ConvertTo`, or `ConvertFrom` methods exist anywhere in the api module (grep across both version packages returns nothing). The two versions co-exist as independent schemas rather than via Kubernetes hub-and-spoke conversion.

Each CRD type self-registers into the controller-runtime scheme via a package-level SchemeBuilder + an `init()` that calls `s.AddKnownTypes(GroupVersion, &T{}, &TList{})`. The exported `AddToScheme` is the single entry point a controller/main wires the whole api group in with (e.g. SandboxAgent registers itself this way).

Validation is enforced in two layers: (1) declarative kubebuilder markers (Enum, MinLength/MaxLength, MinItems/MaxItems, Minimum, Pattern, Required) and CEL `+kubebuilder:validation:XValidation` rules that run at apiserver admission, and (2) a small amount of imperative Go validation (e.g. ValidateSubstrateSandboxAgentSpec) the controller calls for constraints CEL cannot express.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, discriminated-union shape): ModelConfigSpec carries ~16 type-level XValidation rules enforcing provider/sub-config consistency — each provider sub-block (openAI, anthropic, azureOpenAI, ollama, gemini, geminiVertexAI, anthropicVertexAI, bedrock, sapAICore) must be nil unless `self.provider` matches that provider. This is the canonical 'tagged union' admission guard repeated per provider.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, credential/security): ModelConfigSpec admission enforces apiKey wiring invariants — apiKeySecret required if apiKeySecretKey set; apiKeySecretKey required if apiKeySecret set EXCEPT for Bedrock and SAPAICore; apiKeyPassthrough is mutually exclusive with apiKeySecret AND must be false for Gemini/GeminiVertexAI/AnthropicVertexAI; openAI.tokenExchange requires apiKeySecret and is mutually exclusive with apiKeyPassthrough.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, TLS trust): TLSConfig (shared by ModelConfig and RemoteMCPServer) has 3 type-level XValidation rules — caCertSecretKey requires caCertSecretRef and vice-versa (both-or-neither), and disableSystemCAs requires either caCertSecretRef or disableVerify ('trust-nothing config rejects every upstream'). Rules live on the TLSConfig TYPE so every consumer inherits them without re-declaring; a dedicated envtest (tlsconfig_cel_test.go) pins these against a real apiserver to catch silent drops on controller-gen upgrades.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, cross-namespace security — non-obvious): RemoteMCPServerSpec rejects a CA-pinning Secret (spec.tls.caCertSecretRef) together with a cross-namespace-permitting allowedNamespaces (from: All or from: Selector), because the pinned CA Secret is mounted by bare name onto the CONSUMING agent's pod and Kubernetes resolves it in the agent's namespace, not the RemoteMCPServer's — so a CA-pinning server cannot be referenced cross-namespace. It also rejects spec.tls when spec.url has http:// scheme (a TLS opinion contradicts a plaintext URL).

BOUNDARY RULE (imperative, not CEL): substrate-sandbox restrictions that CEL cannot express are enforced in Go by ValidateSubstrateSandboxAgentSpec — when platform==substrate it rejects BYO agents, rejects declarative skills (refs/gitRefs), and forces declarative.runtime to be 'go' (not python). The CEL-expressible subset of these same rules also appears as SandboxAgentSpec XValidation; the Go function is the authoritative enforcement the controller invokes.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, referential-integrity within a spec): McpServerTool requires that every entry in RequireApproval (tools needing human approval) also appears in ToolNames — `self.requireApproval.all(x, has(self.toolNames) && x in self.toolNames)`. A CEL list-comprehension cross-field check, not just a shape guard.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, mutual-exclusion idiom): the 'exactly one of value or valueFrom' XValidation `(has(self.value) && !has(self.valueFrom)) || (!has(self.value) && has(self.valueFrom))` is the repeated codebase convention for inline-value-vs-secret-reference fields — used on ValueRef (common_types.go:146), ToolServer (v1alpha1/toolserver_types.go:95), and AgentHarness credentials (agentharness_types.go:113). Same shape also guards 'exactly one of stdio/sse/streamableHttp' transport selection on ToolServer.

KEY INVARIANT (cross-namespace authorization, runtime): AllowedNamespaces follows the Gateway-API cross-namespace handshake — default (nil receiver) means same-namespace-only; From=All allows all; From=Selector requires a label selector and reads the SOURCE namespace's labels via the kube client. Selector resolution surfaces a distinct error on Forbidden/Unauthorized ('requires namespace read access'), so RBAC gaps fail closed, not open.

KEY INVARIANT (value semantics): TLSConfig.IsEmpty() treats a nil receiver and an all-zero struct as equivalent ('no TLS config supplied'), centralizing the emptiness check in one helper so adding a field only requires updating IsEmpty rather than every caller. This is the load-bearing 'absent vs explicit-default' distinction consumers rely on to fall back to the system trust store.

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE: the module is `github.com/kagent-dev/kagent/go` built on sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime v0.24.1 (kubebuilder-style CRD scheme); tests use stretchr/testify and envtest (a real ephemeral kube-apiserver) to validate CEL admission rules end-to-end. CRD YAML is generated by controller-gen into config/crd/bases/*.yaml (8 CRDs shipped).

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE / DATA-STORE: CRD spec structs double as DB-persistable values — RemoteMCPServerSpec implements `database/sql` Scanner and `driver.Valuer` by JSON-marshalling the whole spec, so the same Go type is stored as a JSON column. The api/database sub-package (a separate concern from the CRD types) persists agents/sessions/events to Postgres via jackc/pgx/v5 with pgvector for embeddings.

DATA MODEL: Agent has two workload shapes behind one shared interface — the AgentObject interface (GetAgentSpec/GetAgentStatus/GetWorkloadMode) is implemented by both Agent (WorkloadMode='deployment') and SandboxAgent (WorkloadMode='sandbox'); SandboxAgentSpec embeds AgentSpec inline and adds Platform (agent-sandbox|substrate). This lets controllers treat the two CRDs polymorphically while reconciling to different workload types.

BOUNDARY RULE (CEL, auth/allowlist): AgentHarness channel auth is admission-guarded — allowlistChannels is required when channelAccess=='allowlist', allowedUserIDs and allowedUserIDsFrom are mutually exclusive, and gatewayToken vs gatewayTokenSecretRef are exactly-one. Channel access is a 3-state enum allowlist|open|disabled. These are the authorization boundaries for the Telegram/Slack chat front-ends.

BEHAVIORAL FACT (provider defaulting): ModelProviderConfig validation is split — a Pattern marker `^https?://.*` plus an XValidation both gate the endpoint, and secretRef is required for all provider types EXCEPT Ollama (which needs no auth). DefaultModelProviderEndpoint hard-codes per-provider defaults (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini/Ollama) and returns empty for Azure/Bedrock/Vertex which require user-specific endpoints; GetEndpoint() falls back to these defaults when Endpoint is blank.

candidate_issues.family: IF-7a (error-path contradicts a documented rule / trusted-input)
candidate_issues.claim: GitRepo.Path documents that the API validates the input path but 'treats repository contents as trusted: symlinks under this path are dereferenced when materializing the skill.' Dereferencing symlinks from a fetched git repo while treating contents as trusted is a documented path-traversal / arbitrary-file-read exposure if any gitRef can come from a less-trusted source. The CRD field carries no symlink-escape guard — the trust assumption is the boundary.

The CEL provider-discriminator string set (Anthropic;OpenAI;AzureOpenAI;Ollama;Gemini;GeminiVertexAI;AnthropicVertexAI;Bedrock;SAPAICore) is duplicated as bare string literals across the +kubebuilder:validation:Enum marker AND ~16 separate XValidation rule strings in modelconfig_types.go, with the v1alpha1 copy enumerating a SUBSET (no Bedrock/SAPAICore). The provider names are magic strings repeated in CEL rule text with no shared constant binding them to the Go ModelProvider consts, so adding a provider requires editing the enum and N rule strings in lockstep — a drift hazard.

candidate_issues.family: IF-6 (divergent allowed-value sets for the same concept)
candidate_issues.claim: The ModelProvider enum diverges between API versions: v1alpha1 allows 7 providers (Anthropic;OpenAI;AzureOpenAI;Ollama;Gemini;GeminiVertexAI;AnthropicVertexAI) while v1alpha2 allows 9 (adds Bedrock;SAPAICore). Two CRD versions naming the same 'provider' concept with different permitted-value sets and no conversion webhook to bridge them can surprise clients that read v1alpha1.

Documented asymmetry: RemoteMCPServer rejects spec.tls when spec.url is http:// (TLS opinion vs plaintext URL), but ModelConfig has 'no equivalent rule, so a TLS block can sit alongside any baseUrl' (remotemcpserver_types.go:86-89). Two consumers of the same TLSConfig type enforce different scheme-vs-TLS consistency, so a ModelConfig can carry a TLS block against a plaintext endpoint without admission rejecting it — a silent misconfiguration window the sibling type guards against.

extractions.component: core/controller+translator (kagent Kubernetes reconciler + ADK agent translator)
extractions.role: The controller-runtime reconciliation + ADK-translation layer of the kagent Kubernetes controller.

facts.claim: The reconciler is built on controller-runtime (sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime v0.24.1); controllers are wired via ctrl.NewControllerManagedBy(mgr) with For(&Agent{}) plus Owns()/Watches() for owned and dependency resources. The whole component is a kubebuilder-style operator, not a service.

The controller's only persistent data store is PostgreSQL, accessed through the database.Client interface (api/database/client.go:22) backed by a pgxpool connection (jackc/pgx/v5 v5.9.2) with pgvector registration. The reconciler writes rendered agent config, tool servers, and discovered tools to this DB; there is no embedded/SQLite store in the production path.
Go toolchain is go 1.26.3 (go.mod). MCP tool discovery uses github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk/mcp; agent-to-agent uses a2a-go; manifest merge uses dario.cat/mergo.

Owned-object reconciliation is idempotent via a custom CreateOrUpdate: it Gets the existing object, applies the per-kind MutateFn, then calls reconcilerutils.ObjectsEqual (a proto-aware DeepEqual that skips ObjectMeta/TypeMeta/Status) and ONLY issues an Update when the relevant fields actually differ — returning OperationResultNone otherwise. This is the load-bearing no-op guard that prevents update churn / reconcile hot-loops on proto-based CRDs that reflect.DeepEqual mis-compares.

Each owned-object create-or-update is wrapped in retry.RetryOnConflict(retry.DefaultRetry, ...), so a 409 Conflict from a concurrent write is retried rather than failing the whole reconcile. A failure on one desired object is collected (errs) and the loop continues to the next object rather than aborting.

Owned-resource garbage collection (pruning) is computed by set-difference: FindOwnedObjects lists all objects owned by the Agent UID (via the .metadata.owner field index), then reconcileDesiredObjects deletes each desired object from that map; whatever remains is pruned with kube.Delete. So an object that drops out of the rendered manifest IS actively deleted on the next reconcile — the desired manifest is authoritative.

The owner-UID field index that FindOwnedObjects depends on is deliberately narrow: SetupOwnerIndexes only indexes objects whose controller owner Kind is exactly 'Agent' or 'SandboxAgent' (an explicit optimization). Owned objects with any other owner Kind are NOT indexed and therefore would not be found for pruning — a constraint a new owner-type would silently violate.

MutateFuncFor merges desired annotations/labels OVER existing (preserving human/other-controller additions) but for Deployment/Sandbox/PodTemplate it REPLACES the spec wholesale (existing.Spec = desired.Spec). mutateSandbox/mutatePodTemplate exist specifically because mergo does not reliably replace slice fields (containers/volumes/env), so without the explicit assignment a spec change would not roll pods. This is a documented correctness workaround copied from the opentelemetry-operator.

mutateDeployment intentionally only sets existing.Spec.Replicas when desired.Spec.Replicas is non-nil, so an unset replicas in the rendered manifest does NOT clobber an HPA-managed replica count. This is a deliberate HPA-compatibility carve-out.

DB tool refresh is atomic via a single transaction: RefreshToolsForServer runs SoftDeleteToolsForServer + per-tool UpsertTool inside withTx (Begin/Commit, deferred Rollback). The reconciler comment at the call site asserts this atomicity. So a partial tool list is never persisted — either all discovered tools replace the prior set or none do.

Status updates are gated to avoid reconcile loops: every status writer (Agent, ModelConfig, ModelProviderConfig, RemoteMCPServer) only calls kube.Status().Update when meta.SetStatusCondition reports a change OR ObservedGeneration/SecretHash/DiscoveredTools differ. RemoteMCPServer explicitly short-circuits with reflect.DeepEqual on DiscoveredTools 'to prevent looping the reconciler'.

Cross-namespace authorization boundary: validateCrossNamespaceReferences enforces that (a) same-namespace tool refs are always allowed, (b) the target namespace must be in the controller's watchedNamespaces, and (c) the target Agent/RemoteMCPServer must opt in via spec.AllowedNamespaces.AllowsNamespace. MCPServer and Service (external types) are HARD-banned from cross-namespace reference entirely. This runs before translation, so an unauthorized ref fails reconcile with a Failed condition.

CRD-level (CEL/kubebuilder XValidation) admission rules enforce invariants BEFORE the reconciler runs, e.g. RemoteMCPServer forbids spec.tls when url is http:// (TLS opinion contradicts plaintext), and forbids cross-namespace allowedNamespaces (from: All/Selector) when a CA Secret is pinned (the Secret resolves in the agent's namespace, not the RMS's). The reconciler's buildRemoteMCPServerTLSConfig explicitly relies on the ModelConfig 'trust-nothing' CEL rule (disableSystemCAs requires caCertSecretRef or disableVerify) being unreachable in production.

Secret-rotation rollout is driven by a content hash, not the Secret name: computeStatusSecretHash hashes (sorted) namespaced-name + sorted data keys + values into Status.SecretHash for ModelConfig and RemoteMCPServer; the translator folds these hashes into the agent's config hash so an in-place CA/cert rotation (same Secret name, new PEM) forces a Deployment roll even though the mount path is unchanged. Without this an agent would keep dialing with stale cached trust.

RemoteMCPServer registration (header resolve + MCP connect + tool list) is bounded by a per-resource timeout (spec.timeout, default 30s) via context.WithTimeout, so a hung/unreachable endpoint cannot block the reconciler goroutine and stall all subsequent RemoteMCPServer reconciliations. On registration failure the reconciler falls back to previously-discovered tools from the DB so a transient outage does not wipe a server's tool list from status.

The agent tool graph is enforced to be a DAG at translate time: validateAgent tracks visitedAgents (kind-qualified keys) and a depth counter with MAX_DEPTH=10; a revisit returns 'cycle detected in agent tool chain' and depth>10 returns 'recursion limit reached'. Self-reference is separately rejected. Cycle/recursion protection lives in the translator, not the CRD.

Reconcile ordering per Agent is fixed: validateCrossNamespaceReferences -> CompileAgent -> BuildManifest -> (optional mutate, e.g. reassign ownership to SandboxAgent) -> reconcileDesiredObjects (apply+prune owned objects) -> upsertAgent (persist rendered config to DB). The DB upsert happens LAST, only after the Kubernetes objects are reconciled, so the DB reflects successfully-applied manifests.

Egress rewrite is gated by a controller flag mcpEgressPlaintext: when on, createMcpTransport rewrites a RemoteMCPServer's https dial URL to http://host:port via egress.RewriteURL so the discovery probe egresses plaintext to a TLS-originating proxy; the agent translator calls the SAME RewriteURL so the controller's discovery dial matches the agent's runtime tool URL exactly. TLS config is still built from spec.tls regardless of the rewrite.

Resource cleanup model is split: Agent/SandboxAgent deletion is handled by observing apierrors.IsNotFound during reconcile (handleDeletedAgentResource deletes the DB row) PLUS Kubernetes owner-reference garbage collection for owned workloads — there is NO finalizer on Agent. By contrast the AgentHarness controller DOES use an explicit finalizer (agentHarnessFinalizer) for ordered cleanup. So Agent DB-cleanup is best-effort on a live controller, not finalizer-guaranteed.

validateRuntimeFeatures implements SOFT validation: when an Agent selects runtime: go but uses Go-unsupported features (executeCodeBlocks, context compaction), it sets an UnsupportedFeatures status condition warning but does NOT fail reconciliation — the features are silently ignored. The Accepted condition still goes True.

Owned-resource controllers run only as leader (controller.Options{NeedLeaderElection: new(true)}) and Create events for owned objects are dropped (typedOwnedObjectPredicate.Create returns false) so the controller does not re-reconcile resources it just created. The Agent itself is reconciled on GenerationChanged or LabelChanged predicates only.

ModelProviderConfig model discovery is on-demand only, never periodic: shouldDiscoverModels returns true only on first reconcile (no LastDiscoveryTime) or a generation change, and updateModelProviderConfigStatus returns ctrl.Result{} with no RequeueAfter. A separate RefreshModelProviderConfigModels entry point is invoked by the HTTP API for forced refresh.

candidate_issues.claim: Agent/SandboxAgent DB-row deletion has no finalizer: handleDeletedAgentResource only runs when the reconciler observes apierrors.IsNotFound for an already-deleted object. If the Agent is deleted while the controller is down (or the delete event is missed and the object is GC'd before a reconcile), the corresponding database.Agent row is never deleted, orphaning the rendered config in Postgres. The owned k8s workloads are still cleaned by owner-ref GC, but the DB is not. The AgentHarness controller uses a finalizer for exactly this guarantee; Agent does not.

In reconcileTranslatedAgent the DB upsert (upsertAgent, storing rendered config) runs only after reconcileDesiredObjects, but reconcileDesiredObjects itself first applies all desired objects and THEN prunes. If pruning fails (deleteObjects returns an error), reconcileTranslatedAgent returns before upsertAgent, so the DB config is not refreshed for that cycle even though new/updated workloads were already applied to the cluster — a window where the live Deployment and the DB-stored config can diverge until a later successful reconcile.

validateRuntimeFeatures only warns (soft validation) when runtime: go is combined with unsupported features (context compaction / executeCodeBlocks); reconciliation still succeeds and the features are silently dropped. An operator who relies on compaction could run an agent that ignores it with only a secondary status condition surfacing the loss — easy to miss since Accepted stays True.

FindOwnedObjects-based pruning silently depends on SetupOwnerIndexes only indexing owners of Kind 'Agent'/'SandboxAgent'. The GetOwnedResourceTypes comment notes that if another controller starts owning resources the method must be updated; the owner index has the same hidden constraint in a different file. A future owner type added without updating both the index Kind filter AND GetOwnedResourceTypes would not be pruned, leaking resources, with no compile-time signal.

buildRemoteMCPServerTLSConfig sets InsecureSkipVerify = spec.tls.disableVerify (gosec G402 suppressed) for the tool-discovery dial, allowing MITM of the discovery probe when an operator sets disableVerify. It is an explicit user opt-in and gated by CEL elsewhere, but it is a real trust-bypass surface on the controller's outbound MCP connection worth flagging for review.

facts.claim: The server is built on gorilla/mux v1.8.1 routing, runs as a controller-runtime Runnable, and wraps the router in otelhttp for OpenTelemetry spans + W3C trace propagation on every request (the /health path is filtered out of tracing).
The HTTP server deliberately runs on ALL replicas, not just the elected leader — NeedLeaderElection() returns false so the API is served everywhere.

Middleware order is load-bearing and the first-registered runs OUTERMOST on incoming requests: wsAuthQueryMiddleware -> AuthnMiddleware -> contentTypeMiddleware -> loggingMiddleware -> errorHandlerMiddleware. Auth therefore runs before logging/error-handling and the wsAuth query->header mapping must run before authentication.

Browser WebSocket endpoints (sandbox SSH, AgentHarness gateway) authenticate via a query-string token because the WebSocket API cannot set headers: wsAuthQueryMiddleware copies ?access_token= (SSH) or ?token= (gateway) into an 'Authorization: Bearer' header before authn, only when no Authorization header is already present.

The default in-tree authenticator (UnsecureAuthenticator) trusts client-supplied identity with NO verification: user id comes from the user_id query param or X-User-Id header, falling back to a hard-coded 'admin@kagent.dev' when absent. This is an insecure-by-default identity path.

The default Authorizer is a no-op: NoopAuthorizer.Check always returns nil, so the Check() authorization wrapper passes for every principal/verb/resource unless a real Authorizer is injected via ServerConfig.

The ProxyAuthenticator parses the JWT payload WITHOUT signature verification by design — it base64-decodes the middle segment and trusts the claims, relying on oauth2-proxy or the k8s service-account layer to have already validated the token upstream. parseJWTPayload only checks the 3-segment shape, never the signature.

Session/event/task/memory data isolation is enforced at the DB query layer by threading userID into every query (GetSession(ctx, id, userID), DeleteSession(ctx, id, userID), ListSessions(ctx, userID)); there is no separate handler-level ownership check — passing the wrong userID simply returns not-found from the DB call.

ModelConfig create writes resources in a specific order to bound a partial-failure window: it Creates the ModelConfig CR first, then creates the API-key Secret, then companion Secrets; if companion-Secret creation fails it rolls back by Deleting the just-created ModelConfig (rollbackOwnerOnCompanionSecretFailure) so an operator retry does not hit AlreadyExists on a half-created resource.

ModelConfig UPDATE uses the opposite ordering rationale from CREATE: it writes Secrets BEFORE flipping the Spec so a partial failure leaves the CR pointing at its prior still-valid Secret layout; new owner-referenced-but-unreferenced Secrets are GC'd when the ModelConfig is eventually deleted. Stale Secrets dropped by the new Spec are swept best-effort (a failed sweep does NOT fail the PUT).

Cross-resource cleanup relies on Kubernetes garbage collection rather than explicit deletes: companion/API-key Secrets are created with an OwnerReference back to the owning CR (Controller=true), so deleting the parent cascades to the Secrets via K8s GC.

Companion-Secret merges enforce two safety invariants before mutating an existing Secret: the existing Secret must be type Opaque, and it must already carry an OwnerReference back to THIS owner (isOwnedBy) — a Secret managed by someone else is never mutated from a different parent's path.

Field-level CRD validation is NOT re-implemented in the httpserver — it is delegated to the Kubernetes API server's CEL/kubebuilder admission rules on the v1alpha2 types (e.g. type must be Declarative|BYO, systemMessage/systemMessageFrom mutually exclusive, MaxItems=20, Enum=python;go). The httpserver only does naming validation (IsDNS1123Label/Subdomain) plus a semantic dry-run.

Agent create/update performs an in-process semantic dry-run validation before persisting: validateAgentObject builds an in-memory kube client wrapper, runs the agent translator's CompileAgent + BuildManifest, and rejects (400 'Invalid agent configuration') if the agent cannot be compiled/translated — catching errors CEL cannot express.

The primary data store behind the session/event/task/memory/feedback/checkpoint handlers is Postgres, accessed through the jackc/pgx/v5 driver behind a database.Client interface; the central error middleware special-cases pgx.ErrNoRows (logged at Info, mapped away from a 500).

Error handling is centralized: handlers never write status codes directly — they call w.RespondWithError(*APIError) and the errorHandlerMiddleware/errorResponseWriter maps the typed APIError.Code+Message to the HTTP response as JSON {"error": ...}. APIError carries a constructor per status (BadRequest=400, NotFound=404, Conflict=409, Validation=422, Forbidden=403, NotImplemented=501, Internal=500).

Authorization is checked before payload validation by convention (toolservers.go documents this explicitly): validateSecretMaterials runs AFTER Check() so an unauthorized caller always gets 403 regardless of payload shape, keeping the error surface dependent only on authz.

The AgentHarness gateway endpoint is a reverse proxy (httputil.ReverseProxy) to a substrate actor via atenet-router (Envoy); it INJECTS its own upstream credentials — a resolved Bearer token and a hard-coded 'x-openclaw-scopes: operator.admin' header — and rewrites Origin/Referer to a loopback value so OpenClaw accepts the browser traffic.

A background MemoryCleanupRunnable prunes expired memory rows on a ticker (default 24h) and DOES require leader election (NeedLeaderElection()==true) to prevent duplicate deletes across replicas — the opposite of the HTTP server's leader policy.

The system startup/boot sequence for the server: NewHTTPServer(config) constructs handlers via handlers.NewHandlers(...) -> Start(ctx) calls setupRoutes() -> wraps router in otelhttp -> ListenAndServe in a goroutine -> a second goroutine waits on ctx.Done() and Shutdown()s with a 5s timeout.

The /health and /version endpoints are effectively unauthenticated reads: while all middleware (incl. AuthnMiddleware) is registered router-wide, the UnsecureAuthenticator default never rejects, and /health is additionally excluded from otel tracing and content-type rewriting. Health uses a raw http.ResponseWriter adapter, bypassing the ErrorResponseWriter contract.

This component is one Go module (github.com/kagent-dev/kagent/go, go 1.26.3); handlers depend on sibling internal packages (controller/reconciler, controller/translator/agent, mcp, a2a, sandboxbackend/substrate) and on the shared go/api packages (httpapi, v1alpha2, database) — it is a dependent (non-leaf) component.

All resource handlers share a Base struct (KubeClient, DefaultModelConfig, DatabaseService, Authorizer, ProxyURL, WatchedNamespaces, SandboxBackend, MCPEgressPlaintext) constructed once in NewHandlers and embedded into every per-resource handler — the single dependency-injection seam for the API layer.

Sandbox (WorkloadModeSandbox) agents enforce a single-session invariant on create: for non-substrate sandbox agents the handler lists ALL users' sessions for that agent and returns 409 Conflict if any already exists ('Sandbox agents support only one chat session').

candidate_issues.claim: The default UnsecureAuthenticator falls back to a hard-coded super-user identity 'admin@kagent.dev' when no user_id/X-User-Id is supplied, and the default NoopAuthorizer authorizes everything. If a deployment is misconfigured to keep these defaults, every unauthenticated request runs as admin with full access. The insecurity is real but appears intentional/named ('Unsecure'); risk is deployment-config-dependent.

ProxyAuthenticator decodes the JWT and trusts its claims (incl. the user-id claim) WITHOUT verifying the signature, relying on an upstream proxy to have validated it. If the server is ever exposed without that trusted upstream in front (or the upstream is bypassed via direct pod access), a caller can forge identity by crafting an unsigned JWT payload. Documented as intentional, but a single-point trust assumption worth flagging.

The AgentHarness gateway reverse proxy (HandleAgentHarnessGateway) performs NO Check(h.Authorizer, ...) authorization call before proxying browser traffic to a substrate actor and injecting operator.admin scopes + a Bearer token — unlike the resource handlers which all gate on Check(). Access control depends entirely on the authn middleware having established a principal; there is no per-resource authorization on the harness identified by {namespace}/{name}.

ModelConfig create spans a Kubernetes CR write and one-or-more Secret writes that are not transactional; the rollback-on-failure for companion Secrets is best-effort (a failed Delete is only logged), and the API-key Secret create failure is logged but NOT surfaced/rolled back (log.Error then continue), so a ModelConfig can persist without its API-key Secret.

The central error writer appends the raw underlying error to the client response: json '{"error": message + ": " + err.Error()}'. Internal error details (DB errors, wrapped translator errors) are echoed to the HTTP client, which can leak internal structure/paths to API consumers.

extractions.role: The PostgreSQL persistence layer for the kagent core controller: a sqlc-generated, pgx/v5-pool-backed repository implementing the dbpkg.Client interface for all controller state — agents, sessions, events, A2A tasks/push-notifications, MCP tools/tool-servers, feedback, LangGraph checkpoints, CrewAI memory/flow-state, and pgvector agent-memory. Owns transaction/atomicity, upsert (ON CONFLICT) semantics, soft-delete conventions, and schema migrations.

facts.claim: Persistence is a sqlc-generated repository: SQL is hand-authored in queries/*.sql and Go is code-generated by sqlc v1.30.0 into gen/ (package dbgen) with sql_package pgx/v5, emit_interface and emit_pointers_for_null_types enabled. Hand-written code never builds SQL strings.

The runtime DB driver is jackc/pgx v5 with a pgxpool.Pool (connection pool); the production client is postgresClient wrapping *dbgen.Queries + *pgxpool.Pool, constructed by NewClient and returned as the dbpkg.Client interface.

ATOMICITY PRIMITIVE: every multi-statement operation goes through withTx(ctx, fn), which begins a pgx tx, runs `defer tx.Rollback(ctx)`, executes fn against a tx-scoped Queries (q.WithTx(tx)), and commits only on fn success. The deferred Rollback after a successful Commit is a harmless no-op — this is the standard pgx commit-or-rollback idiom and is the ONLY atomicity boundary in the layer.

UPSERT SEMANTICS: writes use Postgres `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (<pk>) DO UPDATE` keyed on the table primary key (agent: id; session: id,user_id; tool: id,server_name,group_kind; toolserver: name,group_kind; checkpoint: user_id,thread_id,checkpoint_ns,checkpoint_id; crewai memory: user_id,thread_id) — store is idempotent by PK, last-write-wins on the listed columns, created_at preserved and updated_at set to NOW().

RESURRECTION-ON-UPSERT ASYMMETRY (non-obvious, easy to miss): the agent, tool, toolserver and crewai-memory upserts set `deleted_at = NULL` in their DO UPDATE clause, so upserting a soft-deleted row RESURRECTS it; the SESSION upsert (UpsertSession) does NOT reset deleted_at, so re-storing a soft-deleted session leaves it deleted/invisible. A new engineer will assume all upserts behave alike — they do not.

ATOMIC EVENT+SESSION-TOUCH: InsertEvent is a single-statement CTE — it INSERTs the event and, in the same statement, UPDATEs the parent session's updated_at to NOW() (when session_id is non-null and the session is not soft-deleted). The session 'last activity' bump is therefore atomic with the event insert WITHOUT an explicit transaction.

SOFT-DELETE is the dominant convention: delete = `UPDATE ... SET deleted_at = NOW()`, and EVERY read query carries `deleted_at IS NULL` (Get/List for agent, session, event, tool, toolserver, task, push_notification, checkpoint, crewai flow/memory). Reads of deleted rows are invisible by construction.

SOFT-DELETE EXCEPTIONS: the vector `memory` table and CrewAI `crewai_agent_memory` use HARD DELETE (`DELETE FROM ...`), not soft-delete — DeleteAgentMemory, ResetCrewAIMemory(HardDeleteCrewAIMemory) and DeleteExpiredMemories physically remove rows. SearchAgentMemory's SQL comment explicitly notes memory has no deleted_at filter.

RefreshToolsForServer is delete-then-reinsert INSIDE one transaction: within withTx it SoftDeleteToolsForServer for (server,group) then UpsertTool each tool — so a server's tool set is replaced atomically (no window where the server appears tool-less). The upsert's deleted_at=NULL means a tool that survives the refresh is resurrected rather than duplicated.

READ-WITH-SIDE-EFFECT: SearchAgentMemory is a read that MUTATES — after the vector similarity SELECT it issues IncrementMemoryAccessCount over the returned ids (access_count = access_count + 1). This access_count feeds the TTL/prune policy, so 'searching' memory changes its retention. The two statements are NOT wrapped in a transaction.

MEMORY TTL/PRUNE INVARIANT & ORDER: PruneExpiredMemories runs two statements in ONE transaction in a fixed order — first ExtendMemoryTTL (rows with expires_at<NOW() AND access_count>=10 get expires_at=NOW()+15 days and access_count reset to 0), THEN DeleteExpiredMemories (DELETE rows with expires_at<NOW() AND access_count<10). Order matters: popular rows are rescued before the delete pass; the >=10 vs <10 split is the load-bearing retention threshold.

AGENT-NAME NORMALIZATION (hyphen↔underscore): ListAgentMemories queries BOTH the raw agent name and a `-`→`_` normalized form (agent_name = $1 OR agent_name = $2); DeleteAgentMemory deletes the raw name then, if it differs, the normalized name. This dual-key handling is a non-obvious compatibility shim a caller must respect to avoid orphaned memory rows.

TASK & PUSH-NOTIFICATION DUAL-FORMAT (legacy vs v1): writes still serialize to the legacy trpcv0 format (StoreTask/StorePushNotification with protocol_version=nil); reads dispatch on the stored protocol_version column via parseVersionedTask/parseVersionedPushConfig — nil/empty => legacy (converted up to v1), trpcv0.ProtocolVersionV1 => v1, anything else => hard error. TODO(0.11.0) comments mark this as transitional write-path debt.

CHECKPOINT READ uses an explicit transaction for a multi-query consistent snapshot: ListCheckpoints runs the checkpoint list AND each checkpoint's writes inside one withTx so the (checkpoint, writes) tuple is read consistently; DeleteCheckpoint soft-deletes lg_checkpoint and lg_checkpoint_write together in one tx. Checkpoint ordering is `ORDER BY checkpoint_id DESC`; writes order by (task_id, write_idx).

MIGRATIONS run via golang-migrate (iofs source, pgx/v5 stdlib driver) over two independent tracks with separate version tables: core (schema_migrations) and an optional vector track (vector_schema_migrations), applied by RunUp in core-then-vector order; the vector  gated by checkPgvector (fails fast if the pgvector extension is unavailable).

MIGRATION SAFETY INVARIANT (data-loss guard): on a failed Up, applyDir rolls back ONLY when prevVersion != 0; when prevVersion==0 (no migration ever applied) rollback is deliberately SKIPPED so a failed first migration does not DROP pre-existing GORM-AutoMigrated tables. The same prevVersion==0 guard protects core when a later vector .

MIGRATION DIRTY-STATE RECOVERY: rollbackToVersion handles golang-migrate's dirty flag by Force-ing to the last clean version (currentVersion-1, or -1 to wipe the record entirely if the first migration failed) before running Steps(-n). A dedicated single sql.Open connection (not the pool) is used per migrate instance because the migrate advisory lock is session-level and must not be shared.

SCHEMA ORIGIN: the baseline migration 000001 is explicitly reverse-engineered to match GORM AutoMigrate output as of kagent v0.8.0 — created_at/updated_at are nullable (GORM set them in Go, not via DB default), and version/write_idx/access_count are BIGINT (GORM maps Go int→bigint). New code must treat timestamps as nullable (*time.Time), which is why the gen layer derefs them via derefTime.

USER-SCOPING is an enforced authorization boundary at the query layer: session, event, checkpoint, crewai and memory reads/writes are all keyed on user_id (GetSession requires id AND user_id; ListSessions/ListEventsForSession/ListCheckpoints filter by user_id). A caller cannot read another user's session/events/memory through these queries — there is no cross-user read except the explicit ListSessionsForAgentAllUsers.

pgvector is the vector-search technology: the memory.embedding column is vector(768), search uses cosine distance (`embedding <=> $1`) with COALESCE(1-dist,0) as score, ordered ascending (nearest first); an HNSW index (vector_cosine_ops) backs it and pgvector types are registered per-connection via AfterConnect only when VectorEnabled. memory.id defaults to gen_random_uuid() (added in vector migration 000003).

CONNECT/STARTUP WORKFLOW: Connect → retryDBConnection parses the pool config, optionally wires pgvector AfterConnect, creates the pool, then Pings in an exponential-backoff loop (initial 500ms, doubling, capped 5s) until success or a 120s context timeout; on timeout the pool is Closed and an error returned. URL can be resolved from a file via ResolveURL/resolveURLFile (trims, rejects empty).

CrewAI memory search is parameterized (no SQL injection): SearchCrewAIMemoryByTask builds the LIKE pattern in Go ('%'+taskDescription+'%') and passes it as bind param $3 into `ILIKE $3` (also probing a JSONB task_description field); ordering is created_at DESC then JSONB score ASC. The wildcard is data, not concatenated SQL.

The DBTX seam (Exec/Query/QueryRow) is what makes withTx work uniformly: dbgen.Queries holds a DBTX, and WithTx(tx) returns a clone bound to the pgx.Tx — both *pgxpool.Pool and pgx.Tx satisfy DBTX, so identical generated query methods run either pooled (autocommit) or inside a transaction with no code duplication.

candidate_issues.claim: StoreEvents inserts a variadic batch of events in a plain Go for-loop with NO surrounding transaction — each InsertEvent autocommits independently. If the 3rd of 5 events fails, the first 2 are already committed: the batch is partially persisted and the error gives the caller no way to know which succeeded. Contrast StoreAgentMemories and StoreCheckpointWrites, which DO wrap their loops in withTx. Likely intentional for fire-and-forget event logging, but the inconsistency is a real atomicity gap.

SearchAgentMemory performs the similarity SELECT and the IncrementMemoryAccessCount UPDATE as two separate autocommitted statements (not in withTx). A failure of the increment after a successful search returns an error to the caller even though the search results are valid, and concurrent searches race on access_count (read-modify-write is done in SQL via +1 so the count itself is safe, but the search/increment pair is not atomic). The retention-affecting side effect can silently diverge from what the caller actually consumed.

UpsertSession is the only PK-conflict upsert in the layer that does NOT reset deleted_at=NULL, whereas agent/tool/toolserver/crewai upserts do. Re-storing a previously soft-deleted session silently leaves it deleted (GetSession/ListSessions filter deleted_at IS NULL), so a 'store' can appear to succeed yet the row stays invisible. This divergence from every sibling upsert is undocumented and a likely correctness trap.

DeleteAgentMemory and ListAgentMemories rely on a hyphen→underscore name-normalization compatibility shim, deleting/querying both the raw and normalized agent_name. The two DELETEs in DeleteAgentMemory are autocommitted separately (no transaction): if the second (normalized) delete fails after the first succeeds, memory rows can be left partially deleted / orphaned under the alternate name form.

StoreTask and StorePushNotification still write the LEGACY trpcv0 format with protocol_version=nil even though the read path already supports a v1 format keyed on the protocol_version column. Every new row is written as legacy and converted up on read, so the migration to v1 storage is incomplete; flagged in-code by TODO(0.11.0) comments. Carries ongoing conversion cost and a permanent legacy-compat code path.

facts.claim: sandboxbackend defines two distinct backend interfaces for two CRDs: Backend (synchronous, builds in-cluster Sandbox CRD objects + ComputeReady for the SandboxAgent agent-runtime flow) and AsyncBackend (the minimal Ensure/GetStatus/Delete/OnReady surface a gRPC/HTTP control plane implements to back the AgentHarness CRD). They are deliberately kept separate.

RoutingBackend is the dispatch entry point: it selects the agent-sandbox vs Substrate backend purely from spec.platform. agent-sandbox is the DEFAULT — Substrate is opt-in and only chosen when AgentSandboxPlatform(agent)==SandboxPlatformSubstrate; any other value (including unset) falls through to the AgentSandbox backend.

Substrate state is NOT in a database — it lives entirely in (a) Kubernetes CRDs (ActorTemplate, WorkerPool, the kagent SandboxAgent/AgentHarness) and (b) the external ate-api gRPC Control plane addressed as opaque actor IDs. There is no SQL/ORM anywhere in this component; the only persistent identifier kagent stores is the actor Handle.ID in AgentHarness.Status.BackendRef.

The Substrate actor control plane is reached over a gRPC ate-api Control client (github.com/agent-substrate/substrate ateapipb.ControlClient), TLS-by-default (MinVersion TLS1.2), with an optional per-RPC bearer-token-from-file credential; the dependency is vendored via a go.mod replace to github.com/kagent-dev/substrate v0.0.6.

The whole component is built on sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime v0.24.1 — owner references (controllerutil.SetControllerReference), CreateOrUpdate for upsert, and the client.Object/client.Client abstractions are the load-bearing framework; unit tests use stretchr/testify (require), not Ginkgo.

KEY INVARIANT — EnsureAgentHarness MUST be idempotent: on each reconcile it GetActor first and only CreateActor when the gRPC error is codes.NotFound; an existing actor returns its current handle. This is the reconcile contract spelled out in the AsyncBackend interface doc and implemented identically in both ClawBackend.EnsureAgentHarness and SandboxAgentActorBackend.EnsureSessionActor.

KEY INVARIANT — ActorTemplate reconciliation is an atomic upsert via controller-runtime CreateOrUpdate: the mutate fn overwrites Labels (merged), OwnerReferences, and Spec wholesale to the desired value, so the template self-heals to spec on every reconcile rather than being patched field-by-field.

KEY INVARIANT — actor deletion is a state machine that performs AT MOST ONE mutating ate-api step per call and requires the caller to requeue until done=true: a RUNNING/RESUMING actor is Suspended (not deleted), a SUSPENDING actor is left to settle, and only a SUSPENDED/UNSPECIFIED actor is actually DeleteActor'd. NotFound at any stage is treated as success so the finalizer can be removed idempotently.

KEY INVARIANT — actor IDs are deterministic, DNS-1123-label-safe, and =63 chars, derived from namespace+name (or namespace/name/session). On overflow or an invalid label the code falls back to a sha256-based id (session actors) or a UID-based id (harness/agent actors), so the same logical workload always maps to the same actor id across reconciles.

BOUNDARY RULE — Substrate workload images MUST be pinned by digest: pinImageRef rejects any image ref lacking '@' (no floating tags) before it is written into an ActorTemplate, enforcing reproducible/attestable runsc snapshots. This is an explicit supply-chain boundary, asserted by pinImageRef and covered by a dedicated test that a ':tag' ref is rejected.

BOUNDARY RULE (CRD admission, CEL) — the AgentHarnessSubstrateSpec enforces XOR auth: exactly one of gatewayToken (inline) or gatewayTokenSecretRef (Secret) must be set; and spec.substrate may only be set when runtime==substrate AND is required when runtime==substrate. ResolveGatewayToken in code relies on this CEL guarantee (its comment names it) and resolves the Secret key 'token', failing closed if missing/empty.

BOUNDARY RULE (CRD admission, CEL) — the substrate platform forbids three things at admission: spec.skills is unsupported, spec.substrate may only be set when platform==substrate, and BYO agents (type=='BYO') are rejected. These CEL rules on SandboxAgentSpec are why buildSubstrateGoKagentCommand can hard-code the declarative Go ADK entrypoint without a BYO fallback.

BOUNDARY RULE — the same substrate-only constraints (no BYO, no skills) are re-checked in Go at runtime (ValidateSandboxAgentSpec), not only at CEL admission — defense-in-depth so a bypass of the webhook still fails the reconcile rather than building an unsupported workload.

BOUNDARY RULE — channel credentials (Telegram/Slack tokens) are XOR value-or-valueFrom (CEL, MaxLength 8192) and are never logged; inline values become a literal env var while Secret/ConfigMap sources are passed through as ate-api-resolved secretKeyRef/configMapKeyRef references resolved at actor resume, so kagent's controller never materializes the plaintext secret itself for the Secret path.

BOUNDARY RULE — the OpenClaw/NemoClaw sandbox egress is a default-deny L7 allowlist: only clawhub.ai / openclaw.ai (GET+POST) and docs.openclaw.ai (GET only) are permitted by baseline, each bound to specific binary paths; Telegram and Slack egress are added ONLY when a matching channel is configured. The filesystem policy is read-only by default with a small ReadWrite set (/tmp, /sandbox/.openclaw, etc.) and the process runs as the unprivileged 'sandbox' user/group.

BOUNDARY RULE / WORKAROUND — Substrate ActorTemplate env vars are sanitized to only literal/secretKeyRef/configMapKeyRef; Downward-API fieldRef env (e.g. KAGENT_NAMESPACE) is silently DROPPED. The code therefore prepends KAGENT_NAME/KAGENT_NAMESPACE as literals before the pod env so they win dedup — omitting this makes the Go ADK derive a wrong app name and the controller reject session callbacks with 'Session does not belong to this agent'.

BOUNDARY / RBAC RULE — before reconciling any SandboxAgent the backend calls EnsureAgentSandboxAPIsRegistered, which Lists the agent-sandbox Sandbox CRD (limit 1) and converts a meta.NoKindMatchError into a clear prerequisite error; FilterTranslatorOwnedTypesForList then drops sandbox-only owned types from reconcile Lists on clusters where those CRDs are absent, so a non-sandbox workload never tries to List an uninstalled API.

DEPENDENCY EDGE — the substrate package depends on the openclaw package to build the actor startup: buildOpenClawActorStartup resolves the gateway token, calls openclaw.SubstrateGatewayBootstrap / BuildSubstrateBootstrapJSON, base64-encodes the openclaw.json, and renders it into an embedded shell template (templates/openclaw_startup.sh.tmpl) that becomes the ActorTemplate container command (/bin/sh -c <script>).

ENTRY POINT — the per-session actor lifecycle for a SandboxAgent chat: EnsureSessionActor (GetActor -> CreateActor-if-NotFound -> ResumeActor-if-suspended -> waitForActorReachableViaAtenet), SuspendSessionActor (checkpoint + free the worker), and DeleteAll/Delete session actors; the endpoint surfaced to users is an atenet-router Host header, not a direct pod IP.

BEHAVIORAL FACT — Substrate's atelet copies the ActorTemplate Command verbatim into the OCI spec Process.Args with NO fallback to the image entrypoint, so an empty command makes 'runsc create' fail with 'Spec.Process.Arg must be defined'; this is why the declarative Go command is always set explicitly (host 0.0.0.0, port 80).

BOUNDARY RULE — golden/external actors that Kubernetes garbage collection cannot see are cleaned up explicitly on finalize (CleanupGeneratedTemplate / CleanupSandboxAgentTemplate read Status.GoldenActorID then run the same one-step delete state machine); externally-owned WorkerPools are NEVER deleted by this component, only resolved/read.

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE — for Substrate, ModelConfig API keys are NOT read by kagent; they are passed as env secretKeyRef into the ActorTemplate and resolved by ate-api at actor resume. APIKeyPassthrough is explicitly rejected for the Substrate OpenClaw path (returns an error), and an OpenClaw env SecretRef ({source:env, provider:'default', id:ENVVAR}) is the on-wire representation.

candidate_issues.claim: In deleteActor, the STATUS_SUSPENDING branch ignores the SuspendActor error entirely ('_ = c.SuspendActor(...)'), as does the default branch. A persistent ate-api failure here is silently dropped and the function returns (false, nil), so the reconcile loop will requeue but never surface the underlying error to the AgentHarness status/events — a stuck deletion looks like normal in-progress churn.

SuspendSessionActor swallows all GetActor/SuspendActor outcomes for a nil receiver/client/sa by returning nil, and treats any non-RUNNING/RESUMING/SUSPENDING status as a no-op success. A session actor stuck in an unexpected state is never checkpointed and the worker is silently not freed, with no error returned to the caller.

Config.Insecure sets tls.InsecureSkipVerify=true on the ate-api gRPC client (skipping server cert verification, comment 'same as grpcurl -insecure'). The bearer token is still sent (requireTLS gated on !Insecure), so in the insecure mode the per-RPC bearer credential's RequireTransportSecurity() returns false, allowing the ate-api auth token to ride a connection whose peer identity is unverified. Intended for Kind/local only, but there is no guard preventing Insecure=true in a real cluster config.

ResolveGatewayToken returns the inline sub.GatewayToken trimmed when no secret ref is set, relying ENTIRELY on the CRD CEL XOR rule (gatewayToken xor gatewayTokenSecretRef) for correctness. Unlike the Secret path (which fails closed on empty), the inline path does not re-check that the trimmed token is non-empty before returning it, so a webhook bypass or a whitespace-only inline token yields an empty gateway token silently rather than an error.

The Secret data key for the gateway token ('token') and the per-channel env-var naming scheme (KAGENT_SB_CH_<NAME>_<ROLE>) are magic strings shared across the controller and the runtime workload with no single shared constant bridging both sides; channelSecretEnvVar builds the env key by hand and any drift from the openshell/runtime expectation is undetectable at compile time.

extractions.role: Go library ("ADK") that wires a Google ADK LLM agent into kagent's remote (HTTP/REST) session, task, and memory persistence and exposes it as an A2A (agent-to-agent) server. It owns the agent construction, the per-request execution event loop, session state lifecycle, HITL (human-in-the-loop) tool-approval/resume machinery, and pgvector-backed long-term memory — but it is the runtime/dataplane consumed by the kagent controller, NOT the Kubernetes controller itself.

facts.claim: This component is the kagent ADK agent RUNTIME: it constructs a Google ADK llmagent (CreateGoogleADKAgent) and runs it via google.golang.org/adk/runner inside an A2A server, NOT a Kubernetes reconcile controller. Verified: NO controller-runtime import, NO +kubebuilder/CEL XValidation markers exist anywhere under adk/ (grep returned empty).

Persistence is via the kagent controller's REST API over HTTP, not a direct database connection from this component: sessions POST/GET/DELETE /api/sessions, events POST /api/sessions/{id}/events, tasks POST/GET /api/tasks, memories POST /api/memories/{sessions,search}. The DB (pgvector) is behind that API.

Long-term memory is stored via the Kagent backend API and is documented as backed by pgvector; this component only generates embeddings client-side and POSTs content+vector — it never opens a DB connection itself.

KEY INVARIANT (write durability): event persistence uses a DETACHED context (context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30s)) deliberately so a client disconnect does not cancel the in-flight write to the backend. The request ctx is intentionally NOT propagated to the persist call.

KEY INVARIANT (event-persist ordering / read-your-writes): AppendEvent persists to the backend FIRST, and only on success updates the in-memory localSession (ls.appendEvent) so subsequent reads within the same request see the new event. The order is remote-write-then-local-update.

KEY INVARIANT (partial events excluded from state): localSession.appendEvent returns early and stores NOTHING when event.Partial is true — streaming/partial events never mutate session state or the event log.

KEY INVARIANT (temp state keys are never persisted): before applying an event's StateDelta to session state, keys prefixed with adksession.KeyPrefixTemp are stripped (trimTempDeltaState makes a shallow event copy to avoid mutating the original; updateSessionState skips temp keys again). Temp deltas are request-scoped only.

KEY INVARIANT (concurrency model): localSession state is guarded by a sync.RWMutex; reads of events/state return defensive COPIES (Events() snapshots the slice under RLock; sessionState.All() copies the map under RLock before yielding) so callers cannot mutate or race the live structure.

KEY INVARIANT (idempotent event id): AppendEvent assigns a fresh uuid.New() event ID only when event.ID is empty, so caller-supplied IDs are preserved (enabling de-dup) and only un-ID'd events get a generated one.

KEY INVARIANT (task save is non-mutating + partial-stripping): TaskStore.Save works on a SHALLOW COPY of the task and strips partial streaming events from History and Artifacts (cleanPartialEvents/cleanPartialArtifacts, recognizing three partial-metadata keys incl. legacy ones) before marshaling — the caller's task is never mutated and partials never persist.

BOUNDARY RULE / known limitation: KAgentTaskStore.Save always returns a hard-coded TaskVersion(1) (both Save and Get), so the a2asrv optimistic-concurrency TaskVersion contract is effectively a no-op against the kagent REST backend; List is explicitly unsupported (returns an error).

BEHAVIORAL FACT (system startup / boot workflow): app.New() wires the runtime in order — applyDefaults (port/appName/shutdown) -> if KAgentURL set: start KAgentTokenService, build token-auth HTTP client, create KAgentSessionService + KAgentTaskStore handler opt -> append UserIDCallInterceptor -> EnrichAgentCard -> server.NewA2AServer; Run() blocks until shutdown and stop() tears down the token service.

BEHAVIORAL FACT (per-request execution loop): KAgentExecutor.Execute derives userID/sessionID from the A2A request, ensures a session exists (GetSession-or-CreateSession), optionally rebuilds a HITL resume message, creates a runner, emits Submitted (new task) or Working, ranges over r.Run(...) translating ADK events to A2A status/artifact events, and emits a terminal Completed/Failed/InputRequired/Canceled event.

BOUNDARY RULE (auth / identity propagation): the authenticated user is taken from the x-user-id header via UserIDCallInterceptor (sets callCtx.User); if absent the executor falls back to a synthetic userID of "A2A_USER_"+ContextID. The sessionID is the A2A ContextID. There is no other authorization check in this layer — identity is header-derived.

BOUNDARY RULE (token auth to the controller): outbound calls to the kagent controller carry a Kubernetes projected service-account token read from /var/run/secrets/tokens/kagent-token, refreshed every 60s, injected as 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' plus X-Agent-Name and X-User-Id headers by TokenRoundTripper. Stop() is guarded by sync.Once to prevent a double-close panic on stopChan.

BOUNDARY RULE (Bearer passthrough): the inbound A2A 'authorization: Bearer <tok>' header is extracted (withBearerToken) and stashed in ctx under models.BearerTokenKey for API-key passthrough to model providers, gated separately from the SA-token auth used for controller calls.

BOUNDARY RULE (tool approval / HITL gating): tools whose name appears in any HttpTools/SseTools RequireApproval list are gated by a BeforeToolCallback that, on first invocation, calls ctx.RequestConfirmation and blocks; on re-invocation it proceeds only if ToolConfirmation.Confirmed is true, otherwise returns a rejection result. Approval callback is wired FIRST (before the logging callback).

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE: the agent engine is Google's ADK (google.golang.org/adk — agent/llmagent, runner, session, memory, tool) and the transport is the A2A protocol (github.com/a2aproject/a2a-go a2asrv/a2a). This component is an adapter layer between kagent's REST persistence and Google ADK + A2A.

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE (multi-provider LLM): CreateLLM is a type-switch over 10 provider configs (OpenAI, AzureOpenAI, Gemini, GeminiVertexAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Bedrock via the Converse API for ALL models incl. Anthropic, GeminiAnthropic=Claude-on-Vertex, SAPAICore), each resolving keys/region from specific env vars and defaulting model names (Gemini gemini-2.0-flash, Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-20250514, Ollama llama3.2).

TECHNOLOGY CHOICE (logging): structured logging is logr (go-logr) over a production zap logger (zapr), with ISO8601 timestamps; ctx-scoped loggers via logr.FromContextOrDiscard are used throughout. No DB/ORM is present in this component.

DEPENDENCY EDGE / wiring: runner.CreateRunnerConfig is the assembly point — it builds the ADK agent (injecting a save_memory tool when a memory service is present and an STS token-propagation plugin when KAGENT_PROPAGATE_TOKEN/STS_WELL_KNOWN_URI are set), and falls back to adksession.InMemoryService() when no KAgentSessionService is provided. appName 'NS__'-encoding maps namespace/name to the ADK agent identity.

BEHAVIORAL FACT (graceful-degradation pattern): memory SearchMemory returns EMPTY results (never an error) when the query embedding cannot be generated, and AddSessionToMemory falls back to RAW content when LLM summarization fails or yields empty/invalid JSON — read-path memory failures are non-fatal to the agent run.

BEHAVIORAL FACT (parity with Python): the Go runtime is an explicit port of a Python kagent-adk implementation — multiple methods are documented as mirroring Python (KAgentSessionService.get_session/append_event, _approval.py make_approval_callback, _hitl_utils.py HitlPartInfo, KAgentConfig app-name convention) — so behavior is constrained to match the Python reference, not freely chosen.

KEY INVARIANT (state-tagging on create): session state keys 'session_name' (StateKeySessionName) and 'source' (StateKeySource, from the x-kagent-source header) are propagated into the CreateRequest body (name/source) and stored so the session is tagged in the controller DB; these are the only two state keys lifted into create.

candidate_issues.claim: In KAgentSessionService.Get, when an individual event fails to JSON-unmarshal it is logged at V(1) and SILENTLY SKIPPED (continue), so a corrupt/forward-incompatible event payload is dropped from the reconstructed session history with no surfaced error and no count of dropped events — partial silent data loss on read.

app.New() logs but does NOT fail when tokenService.Start() errors ('Failed to start token service'); it then builds the token-injecting HTTP client and continues, so the app can come up with an empty bearer token and every subsequent authenticated call to the controller will 401/403 at runtime rather than failing fast at startup.

The A2A↔backend wire contract is expressed as duplicated string literals across components without a shared constant: taskstore inlines metadataKey* partial-flag strings with a comment 'inlined to avoid import cycle' (store.go:16) while a2a uses its own KAgent* partial keys, and HTTP header names ('X-User-ID' vs 'X-User-Id', 'x-user-id', 'x-kagent-source', 'authorization') appear as bare literals in session.go, auth/token.go and executor.go. A rename on one side silently breaks cross-component recognition.

KagentMemoryService.AddSessionToMemory stores each summarized fact in a sequential loop and returns on the FIRST storeMemory error (kagent_service.go:122), leaving earlier facts persisted and later ones not — a partial-write with no transactional rollback or all-or-nothing guarantee across the memory items derived from one session.

messageToGenAIContent / extractSessionName derive userID and session name directly from caller-controlled A2A input (x-user-id header, first text part) with no validation or sanitization beyond a 20-char truncation; userID flows verbatim into URL query params (url.QueryEscape is applied in session.go, but it is also concatenated into 'A2A_USER_'+ContextID and used as a DB partition key for memory), so an unauthenticated caller fully controls the identity/partition under which memories and sessions are stored.

score.notes:
DOC-COVERAGE 9/36 = 25% (held-out, CURRENT RCs); DOC-FAITHFULNESS 9/9 = 100% on overlapping facts; deepinit_wrong_high = 0 (hard gate PASS — no code-refuted confident claim; deep-init correctly says PostgreSQL, which is why the one STALE SQLite doc-claim was pre-dropped from the key and is not in scope).

CEL-VALIDATION BOUNDARY-RULES (a key axis): MIXED. Deep-init scored its single strongest hit on the ModelConfig/TLS CEL surface — RC-016 (provider enum, all 9 incl. SAPAICore) and RC-017 (provider-block-nil-unless-matches, apiKeyPassthrough/apiKeySecret mutual exclusion, passthrough-disallowed for Gemini/GeminiVertexAI/AnthropicVertexAI, caCertSecretRef/caCertSecretKey both-or-neither) are precisely covered, plus deep-init went BEYOND the doc with the RemoteMCPServer CA-pinning cross-namespace rule and the ~16-rule discriminated-union framing. BUT it MISSED the Agent-CRD CEL family entirely: RC-014 (type Declarative|BYO, matching sub-spec, systemMessage/systemMessageFrom exclusive, serviceAccountName/serviceAccountConfig exclusive) and RC-015 (McpServerTool requireApproval ⊆ toolNames subset rule). So CEL coverage is concentrated on ModelConfig/TLS and absent on Agent.

KEY-INVARIANTS (the other key axis): WEAK against the doc. Of the doc's 8 key-invariant/atomicity RCs (RC-006, RC-009, RC-010, RC-011, RC-023, plus ordering RC-019-adjacent), deep-init matched only RC-009 (ON-CONFLICT DO UPDATE atomic upserts — explicitly stated). It MISSED RC-006 (Session.Source 'user'/'agent' + agent-sourced exclusion), RC-010 (tool-refresh delete+insert wrapped in one tx — deep-init describes withTx generally and names StoreAgentMemories/StoreCheckpointWrites but not RefreshToolsForServer), RC-011 (MCP network I/O kept OUTSIDE the DB tx), RC-023 (config baked into a Secret mounted at /config, read as config.json at startup). Notably, deep-init's OWN beyond-doc invariants are rich and correct (idempotent CreateOrUpdate no-op guard, set-difference pruning, secret-hash rollout, DAG cycle/depth-10 limit, withTx as the sole atomicity boundary, soft-delete + UpsertSession resurrection asymmetry, memory TTL ExtendTTL-before-Delete, migration prevVersion==0 guard, detached-ctx event persistence) — they just don't line up with the doc's chosen invariants.

WHOLE FAMILIES MISSED — the doc is heavily weighted toward the Python ADK agent-runtime + prompt/template + A2A-subagent surface, which deep-init's CODE-ALONE pass barely touched: RC-022/023/024/025/026 (Go-ADK→Python-ADK three-type-system flow, config.json delivery, text/template prompt resolution with include(), promptTemplate vars, the kagent-builtin-prompts Helm ConfigMap), RC-028/029/030/031/032/033/034 (the entire HITL/A2A-subagent Python layer: before_tool_callback/request_confirmation, KAgentRemoteA2ATool/Toolset, _SubagentInterceptor headers, A2aAgentExecutor session map, the ADK→A2A event converter, the unconditional ask_user tool). Deep-init only grazed this with a generic 'HITL tool-approval' role attribution (RC-028 matched thinly). Also missed: the entry-point/port surface (RC-001 8083, RC-002 A2A-proxy-not-LLM, RC-003 :8080 pod URL), the multi-controller-shared-reconciler enumeration + ConfigMap watch + event predicate (RC-007/012/027), the common-types component (RC-020 ValueRef/TypedReference), session-visibility routes (RC-035), and the executeCodeBlocks-ignored quirk (RC-037).

WHY LOW: deep-init analyzed a DIFFERENT (and largely valid) slice — sandbox/Substrate egress + insecure-by-default auth + DB atomicity internals — that is almost entirely beyond-doc, while the reference doc emphasizes the agent-runtime/A2A/prompt-template/CRD-CEL story. Where the two overlap (Postgres store, reconciler composition, ON-CONFLICT upserts, the ModelConfig/TLS CEL rules, the Agent/ModelConfig/RemoteMCPServer CRDs, the translator's ADK role), agreement is exact (9/9 faithful). Strict scoring, no near-miss credit: RC-002 (no explicit A2A-proxy-to-pod role), RC-010 (generic withTx ≠ the specific tool-refresh tx), RC-020 (AllowedNamespaces appears incidentally but not the common-types component/ValueRef/TypedReference) were all withheld.
